segments.s740-g65: همان before the verb is a demonstrative/pronoun referring back to previously mentioned people (’those same’). می‌فرستاد is the imperfect/habitual past (literary) ‘used to send’ or ‘would send’. و simply coordinates the next noun phrase; رامشگران is a plural noun (‘attendants/comforters/peacemakers’) that is linked to the previous clause (either as another thing he sent or as the agents who acted on the palace).
segments.s740-g66: همه modifies کاخ = ‘the whole/entire palace.’ کاخ دینار is a genitive-like descriptive phrase ‘palace of dinars’ (i.e. wealthy palace). بد here is ambiguous in the manuscript; in context it functions adjectivally/copuled to indicate possession or state (classical/poetic contraction often read as ‘was/having’), so the whole phrase means something like ‘a palace full of dinars.’ بی‌کران is an adjective after the noun phrase meaning ‘boundless/without limit.’
segments.s740-g67: به هنگامشان is a time phrase: به + هنگام (’at the time of’) with enclitic شان ‘their’ → ‘at their time/season.’ رامش و خورد is a coordinated noun phrase ‘tranquility and food/sustenance’ (رامش = tranquility; خورد here functions as a noun ‘food’ in older/poetic usage). بود is the past copula ‘was/there was.’
segments.s740-g68: This is a simple nominal sentence with the copula at the end. نگهبان (guard/guardian) is the topic/subject, ایشان is a possessive/third‑person plural ‘their,’ چهل مرد is the predicate noun phrase ‘forty men,’ and بود is the past copula ‘were/was’ tying subject and predicate together. Word order is Topic — Possessor — Predicate — Copula (typical in literary Persian).
segments.s740-g57: همی‌برگرفتند is a classical/poetic imperfective past form with the prefix همی- indicating a repeated or continuous past action: 'they kept taking' or 'they would take'. زیشان is a contraction of ز ایشان (preposition ز 'from' + pronoun ایشان 'them'), marking source. شمار is the noun 'number/count' and is the object of برگرفتند: 'they took/count(ed) the number from them.'
segments.s740-g58: که here introduces a clause of explanation/cause ('for, because'); پرسه is a noun used here to name the kind of people/thing whose quantity is given (poetic/archaic usage, 'prowlers/loiterers' in this lexicon); فزون آمد is a periphrastic expression meaning 'became more / increased' (فزون 'more' + آمد 'came/became'); از سه هزار gives the measure 'beyond three thousand.' Together: 'for the X increased beyond three thousand.'
segments.s740-g59: همه کاخها is a simple noun phrase 'all the palaces.' رایک (رایک) is an abstract noun 'one opinion/one counsel' and اندر دگر ('in/into another') indicates separation or contrast among them; the phrase literally conveys that each palace took its own (separate) counsel or decision.
segments.s740-g60: برید here functions as a past verb meaning 'took/assumed' (from بردن sense) — 'they took/held (that opinion/course)'. آنک is the relative/demonstrative 'that which' or 'what/that'. بد qualifies the following idea as 'bad/ill' (i.e. harmful). شاه را uses the object/topic marker را with 'king' to indicate whom the bad thing concerns ('for the king' or 'to the king's detriment'). کارگر is an adjective meaning 'effective/operative' (so 'that which was bad for the king proved effective'). The clause therefore says that the course harmful to the king actually succeeded/was effective.
segments.s740-g61: ز (poetic form of از) is the preposition 'from.' پوشیدنیها is a plural noun derived from پوشیدن meaning 'wearable items / clothes'; و is the conjunction 'and.' از + خوردنی repeats the preposition with خوردنی ('edibles/food'). The construction is a simple enumerative list introduced by ز/از.
segments.s740-g62: The prepositional pattern continues: ز بخشیدنی (from that which is given/grants — بخشیدنی = 'things to be given' or 'gifts') + هم 'also' + ز گستردنی ('from things to be spread' — گستردنی is a deverbal adjective 'that which is spread/for spreading'). These deverbal adjectives are used nominally in a list of items or provisions.
segments.s740-g63: به is the preposition 'to/at/for'; ایوانهاشان is 'their iwans/porticoes' (ایوان + plural + possessive suffix). بیاراستند is the past third-person plural of بیاراستن 'to adorn/arrange' ('they adorned/ornamented'). The verb follows the (prepositional) object, a common order in Persian.
segments.s740-g64: پرستنده and بندگان are two coordinated nouns: پرستنده (literally 'worshipper' or 'devotee' but here plausibly 'attendant/devoted follower') and بندگان 'servants/servitors.' خواستند is the past tense 'they wanted/sought' and here can be read as 'they sought/engaged attendants and servants.' The verb is plural to agree with the implicit subject from the previous clause.
segments.s740-g49: بیازرد here functions as a negative/resultative predicative — roughly 'was not harmed / did not hurt (him)'. ز is the poetic prepositional clitic (از) meaning 'from / by', and شهریار بزرگ is the noun phrase 'the great king' placed after the verb for poetic emphasis. Word order is verb + agent/prepositional phrase + nominative noun for emphasis.
segments.s740-g50: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for / who'. کودک جوان بود is a copular clause ('was a young child') with بود as the past copula. و links the second predicate; گشته is a perfect/participial form 'has become / turned', followed by سترگ as the adjectival/complementive element. Together the clause explains why the king did not harm him.
segments.s740-g51: پر از + noun is an adjectival construction meaning 'full of X' (here 'full of pain'). شد is the verbal predicate 'became' or 'was made'; جان خندان is a noun + adjectival phrase 'his laughing/smiling life/soul' (خندان modifying جان). اوی is the possessive/3rd‑person clitic 'his' referring back to the subject.
segments.s740-g52: This clause is coordinated by the narrative conjunction (here translated 'and'); ایوان او is a genitive phrase 'his hall/portico', کرد is the past tense of 'to make/do' used here in the causative/active sense 'made/turned', and زندان اوی is the object 'his prison'. The verb follows its object phrase(s) and the clitic او/اوی marks possession.
segments.s740-g53: هم آن را is a topical/emphatic phrase 'also/that very one' (هم adding 'also' or emphasis). که introduces a relative clause; پیوسته is an adverb 'continuously/constantly', and اوبدند is an older/poetic plural past form meaning 'they were / they dwelt/they remained' — here describing people who had been continually attached to him.
segments.s740-g54: گه = 'at times / sometimes' qualifies the following verb. رای جستن is an infinitival noun+verb combination 'to seek counsel' (رای = counsel/plan, جستن = to seek). براو is the prepositional phrase 'to him/upon him', and شدند is the past plural 'they became / they were' used here as an intransitive copula 'they turned to / they went to'.
segments.s740-g55: بسی دیگر is a prefatory expression 'many others' (بسی used poetically for 'many/much'), followed by از + noun phrase 'of/among the ...'. مهتر و کهتران is a paired noun phrase 'the elders/chiefs and the younger/inferior ones' (parallel ranks). Word order places the quantifier before the 'of' phrase.
segments.s740-g56: که introduces the relative clause 'who', بودند is the past plural copula 'they were', با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him', and ببندگران is an agentive plural noun meaning 'binders/keepers/wardens' — i.e. those who served as attendants or guards. The clause identifies those many others as attendants of the subject.
segments.s740-g25: بدست چپ is a prepositional/locative phrase: بدست here means “in the hand,” and چپ specifies which hand (“left”). The possessor phrase آن جوان سترگ follows the possessed noun (دل‑style genitive order common in older Persian), so the whole chunk reads “in the left hand of that young (man) …”.
segments.s740-g26: This is a noun phrase describing what was in the hand. بریده is a past participle/adjective “cut off, severed” modifying چنگال; یکی is the numeral “one/a”; خشک is an adjective “dry”; چنگال is the head noun “claw”; گرگ after the noun serves as a genitive/possessor “of the wolf.” Word order is adjective(s) + noun + possessor, typical of older Persian poetic style.
segments.s740-g27: This is a compact descriptive clause. سروی is the noun “cypress”; سر گاومیشی is a descriptive/genitive phrase “(with) a buffalo‑like head” modifying سروی; براست is a predicate/adverb “upright” or “stood upright.” In poetic Persian the copula is often omitted and براست supplies the stative sense.
segments.s740-g28: همی is a focusing adverb (“just/now/indeed”); این is the demonstrative object “this”; بران indicates direction/target “upon that”; بر is the preposition “on/upon” used with the verb; زدی is the past tense of زدن “to strike” (he/one struck). چونک introduces the subordinate causal/temporal clause “as/when” and خواست is the past tense “he wished/desired.” So the clause says he struck this upon that when he wished.
segments.s740-g29: غمی شد is a verbal phrase “became sorrow/was grieved” where شد is the past of شدن ‘become’ and غمی is the predicate; دل موبد is the logical subject (‘the mobed’s heart’) following the verb for poetic emphasis. از introduces the causal phrase “because of,” and کاراوی here refers to the relevant deed/feat (an effective act), rendered as a noun (the effective thing).
segments.s740-g30: ز is the causal/prepositional particle “because of” (poetic form). بازی و بیهوده کردار is a coordinated noun phrase “play and futile (or pointless) conduct,” where بیهوده adjectivally modifies کردار. اوی is the third‑person possessive/pronoun attached after the noun meaning “his” (his conduct).
segments.s740-g31: به فالش آمد is an idiomatic construction meaning “to appear as an omen” (به فال آمدن). بد qualifies the omen as unfavorable (“bad”/unfavorable). هم adds “also/too,” and آن چنگ گرگ is the topical subject “that wolf‑claw” which is being described as the omen.
segments.s740-g32: This is a coordinated noun phrase giving what the bad omen affects. شخ گاو is a compound whose first element (شخ) is uncertain/obscure here (possibly a dialectal/poetic word for a part of/attribute of the ox); و coordinates it with رای جوان سترگ, where رای means “plan/counsel” and is possessed by جوان سترگ (“the young/brave man”). The possessor follows the possessed noun, as in older Persian.
segments.s740-g9: این صدرِ جمله (word order: prepositional phrase + predicate) places 'ازان چاره' ('of/from that plan/means') first for emphasis. 'آگه نبد' is an older negative past construction meaning 'was not aware' (نبد = was not / did not exist), and 'هیچ‌کس' is the indefinite subject 'no one' after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s740-g10: 'که' introduces the content clause 'that he had...'. 'او داشت' is simple past ('he had'). 'آن راز' = 'that secret' (آن = that). 'تنها و بس' is an idiomatic pair: 'تنها' = alone/solely and 'بس' = enough/sufficient; together they emphasize exclusivity and sufficiency ('he alone had the secret, and that was enough').
segments.s740-g11: 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. 'سالی برآمد' is a stock phrase meaning 'a year came/arrived' (برآمد = arose/occurred). The following 'که مریم بمرد' is a subordinate clause specifying that year; بمرد here expresses the event 'that Mary died' (classical subjunctive/past sense used as a factual past).
segments.s740-g12: This is a main clause with verb-final order typical of classical Persian: 'شبستان زرین' (the golden bedchamber) is the object/topic, 'به شیرین' marks the beneficiary ('to/for Shirin'), and 'سپرد' is the past transitive 'entrusted/handed over'.
segments.s740-g13: 'چو' = 'when' introduces another temporal clause. The sequence 'شیرویه را سال شد' uses the object/experiencer marker 'را' with 'سال شد' ('became a year' = reached the age of) — an idiomatic way to say someone turned a certain age. 'بر دو هشت' specifies that numerical age (poetically phrased).
segments.s740-g14: The phrase 'به بالا' = 'and upwards/beyond' modifies the age just given; 'زسی' is an archaic/obscure particle here indicating 'from that' or continuation, 'سالگان' is the plural noun 'years/ages', and 'برگذشت' is the past intransitive 'passed by'. Together the segment says that additional years passed beyond that point.
segments.s740-g15: Word order is inverted for poetic effect: the verb 'بیاورد' ('brought/produced') begins the clause, 'فرزانگان را' is the definite object 'the sages/learned men' marked by 'را', and 'پدر' (father) appears post-verb as the logical subject — a common flexible order in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s740-g16: The unit 'بدان' here (written in the data as 'ب' + 'دان') functions as a phrase meaning 'for that / to that end' introducing purpose; 'تا' also marks purpose ('so that / in order that'), followed by 'شود' (subjunctive/potential 'may become' or simple future 'become'). 'نامور پر هنر' is a predicate: 'نامور' = renowned and 'پر هنر' = 'full of art/skill' (پر = full of).
segments.s740-g1: ازانِ پس is a temporal phrase: ازان (poetic از آن) 'thereupon/after that' + پس 'after/then', marking sequence. فزون شد is the verbal predicate: فزون 'increasing' + شد 'became/grew' (past), and بزرگی شاه is the noun phrase 'the greatness of the king' with شاه as the possessor following the abstract noun (noun + possessor order).
segments.s740-g2: که introduces the result/consequence clause ('so that' / 'such that'). خورشید شد is the main verb of the clause 'became the sun' (خورشید 'sun' + شد 'became'). آن کجا بود ماه is a poetic, inverted locative clause 'the place where the moon was' (آن کجا = 'that where/there', بود = past 'was', ماه = subject 'moon'), i.e. 'where the moon had been'.
segments.s740-g3: همه روز is an adverbial phrase 'all day'. با is the preposition 'with' marking accompaniment; دخت قیصر is a genitive construction 'the daughter of the Caesar' (دخت as 'daughter/maiden' followed by possessor قیصر). بدی here is an archaic/poetic past verbal form meaning roughly 'he spent/kept [time with her]' (the dictionary senses given in the data don't match a verbal sense, so this is an inferred verbal use).
segments.s740-g4: همو 'the same one' (= her) is a poetic object pronoun referring back to the daughter. بر شبستانش uses بر + شبستان (شبستانش = 'his bedchamber' with -ش possessive) to indicate role/position 'over/in respect to his bedchamber'. مهتر is the noun 'chief/head' and the final بدی is the same archaic past verb as above, here meaning 'he made/appointed (her) [as] chief'; word order places the object pronoun up front for emphasis.
segments.s740-g5: ز مریم here functions as 'because of Maryam' (ز as 'because of/from' in poetic use). همی‌بود is an imperfective/past habitual 'would be' or 'used to be'. شیرین is the proper name Shirin, and بدرد is the intransitive/causative sense 'to be in pain / to suffer' (here 'be pained'), so the clause reads 'Because of Maryam, Shirin would be pained.'
segments.s740-g6: همیشه 'always' sets a generalizing adverb. ز رشکش is 'from/because of his jealousy' (رشک + possessive -ش). دو رخساره (poetically written here) = 'two-faced / two-cheeked' (i.e. both cheeks/faces); رخساره means 'face/complexion'. زرد 'yellow' in classical Persian often means 'pale/wan', so 'both her faces became pale from his jealousy.'
segments.s740-g7: به فرجام is a temporal/clinching phrase 'in the end / finally'. شیرین is the indirect object (Shirin) and ورا ('to her') is the enclitic object pronoun reinforcing the indirect object. زهر is the direct object 'poison' and داد is the past verb 'gave' — together: 'in the end he gave Shirin poison.'
segments.s740-g8: شد here is a short past verb 'became' used elliptically/poetically to mean 'came to be / perished' in context; آن نامور is 'that famed/renowned', and دخت قیصرنژاد is a titular description 'daughter of Caesar's line' (قیصرنژاد 'of imperial stock'). Together they state that the famed daughter of the Caesar's house was thus lost/was no more.
segments.s740-g41: ز here is the poetic causal preposition 'because of'. The phrase treats رنگ رخش as a unit: رنگ (complexion/appearance) qualifies رخش (his visage/face); classical Persian often omits the ezāfe, so رنگِ رخش is compressed to 'رنگ رخش'. شد is the past/resultative 'became' (i.e. 'grew pale').
segments.s740-g42: ز again marks cause ('because of'). کار زمانه functions as a compound meaning 'the dealings/ways of the times/the world' (no linking particle). پراز is an adjective 'full (of)'; درد here is emotional sorrow; شد is the past 'became' (result: 'became full of sorrow').
segments.s740-g43: ز = 'because of' or 'from'. گفتار means 'speech/words'. The sequence مرد ستاره شمر is appositive/compound without an explicit ezāfe (typical in epic verse) and should be read as 'the man called Setāra-Shamr' (a name/epithet).
segments.s740-g44: دلش = دل + enclitic pronoun ش ('his heart'). بود is the past copula 'was'. پر + [noun] = 'full (of ...)'; here پر درد = 'full of pain/sorrow'. و = 'and'. پیچان (a participial/adjectival form) modifies جگر = 'liver' used metaphorically as the seat of feeling; the phrase means his inner organs convulsed / his heart/liver twisted with grief.
segments.s740-g45: همی‌گفت is the habitual/past iterative 'kept saying' or 'would say'. تا introduces the subordinate clause (classical use: 'that/asking how'), and کردگار سپهر is the subject phrase 'the Maker (Creator) of the heavens' (سپهر = sky/heavens).
segments.s740-g46: چگونه = 'how'. نماید is the classical present/subjunctive-form verb meaning 'would show/appear/do' (here: 'could/has made appear'). ب functions as the prefix/preposition forming بدین = 'in/with this' (poetic contraction), followed by کرده چهر = 'made face/appearance' (کرده = 'made/done', چهر = 'face/appearance'). Note: the printed 'دین' is part of the compound 'بدین' (in this way); its separate lemma senses do not apply here.
segments.s740-g47: چو = 'when'. بر is the preposition 'upon/over' (so بر پادشاهی = 'upon his kingship'). پادشاهیش is 'his kingship' (poetic attachment of the possessive); بیست وسه سال = 'twenty‑three years' (وسه = 'and three' fused to indicate 'and three' after twenty).
segments.s740-g48: گذر کرد is a verbal phrase 'had passed' (گذر = passing/time, کرد = did/made → 'passed'). The name شیرویه is the actor of the following action; بفراخت is a past action 'roused/raised' (here 'flared up'), and یال = 'mane' (a common epic image for rousing one's pride or fury). Word order in poetry allows the actor's name to appear between verbs.
segments.s740-g33: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and combines with the genitive noun phrase کار زمانه ('the doings/affairs of the age'). غمی is the noun 'a grief' (indefinite with the -ی suffix). گشت is the past of شدن 'to become', so غمی گشت سخت literally 'a grief became severe' — note the predicative adjective سخت follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s740-g34: ازان 'from that' refers back to the previous clause. برمنش کودک is an adjectival + noun phrase where the adjective برمنش ('noble‑minded') precedes کودک ('child') as a poetic ordering. شور بخت is a nominal combination ('turmoil/tumult of fate') and here there is an implied verbal link to the previous گشت — the second clause is elliptical: 'from that ... (he) became [in] شور بخت' (i.e. his fate was thrown into turmoil).
segments.s740-g35: کجا 'where' is fronted to introduce a rhetorical question. طالع زادنش is a genitive/noun phrase 'the fortune (or horoscope) of his birth' (زادن here conveys 'birth'). دیده بود is the pluperfect construction: دیده (past participle 'seen') + بود (auxiliary 'had/was'), so 'had been seen' / 'had one seen'.
segments.s740-g36: ز introduces the source 'in/from' the following named works: دستور و گنجور (two titles/authoritative sources). بشنیده بود is again the pluperfect (بشنیده past participle 'heard' + بود auxiliary 'had'), so 'had (one) heard (it) from the Dastur and the Ganjur?'.
segments.s740-g37: سوی موبد is the prepositional phrase 'toward/to the mobed'. The next موبد is the subject 'a mobed' (so the repetition is not a duplication but first marks the goal, second the agent). آمد is the past 'came' and بگفت is the classical past form 'and said' (ب‑ prefix + گفت).
segments.s740-g38: که introduces the reported clause. بازیست is an archaic/persianized present/stative 'it is a play/wonder/spectacle' (here idiomatically 'it is a marvel/strange thing'). باآن is the prepositional 'with that', followed by the adjective گرانمایه 'precious' modifying جفت 'pair'. The clause thus says 'that is a wonder with that precious pair.'
segments.s740-g39: بشد زود is a temporal comment 'it happened soon/quickly' (بشد here = 'it happened/it became'). Then موبد بگفت آن به شاه is the main clause: 'the mobed said that to the king' — آن is the demonstrative object 'that' and به شاه marks the indirect object 'to the king'.
segments.s740-g40: همی‌داشت is the imperfect/progressive 'was having/kept'. خسرو is the subject (the king Khosrow). مر is the archaic enclitic 'me' (beneficiary), او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks the object), and نگاه is the noun 'gaze' — together forming نگاه داشتن 'to keep (in) gaze', so 'Khosrow was keeping his gaze on him for me' (i.e. 'Khosrow kept him in view on my behalf').
segments.s740-g17: همی‌داشت is the imperfect/continuous past-like form: همی- (continuous) + داشت (from داشتن, 'to have/keep'), here meaning 'kept' or 'kept on holding'. The verb appears before its subject (موبد), a common order in epic Persian. نگاه is the noun 'gaze' which behaves as the object of داشتن. مر is an archaic/poetic pronoun (variant of مرا / to me) used enclitically here; او را is the explicit object 'him' (را marks it as definite).
segments.s740-g18: شب و روز is an idiom 'night and day' = continuously; شادان is an adjective/adverb 'joyful/rejoicing' describing the state of the implied subject (the mobad). به فرمان شاه is a prepositional phrase 'by/at the command of the king' (به + noun + possessor), where به indicates agency/cause ('by/at the order of'). Word order places the adverbial time phrase before the causal phrase.
segments.s740-g19: چنان بد که is a fixed classical sequence meaning roughly 'so (it was) that' or 'such that', introducing a subordinate clause. یک روز is a temporal phrase 'one day'; موبد is the subject; ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and تخت is 'throne/seat' — together ز تخت indicates motion from the throne/seat.
segments.s740-g20: بیامد is past tense 'came' (here with the subject carried over from the previous segment). به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near' (به + نزدیک) expressing approach; آن is the demonstrative 'that'; نیک‌بخت is a noun 'fortunate (one)' referring to the person approached. The clause thus states the mobad came close to that fortunate man.
segments.s740-g21: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; آمد is past 'came'. به نزدیک + شیرویه is 'near Shiruyeh' (به نزدیک = toward/near). باز at the end of this clause is an adverb 'again' (it modifies آمد, giving 'came again').
segments.s740-g22: همیشه is 'always'. به introduces the complement 'بازیش' (بازی + -ش, here 'his باز / his hawk' or 'his falconry'), so به بازیش = 'for his hawk' / 'for his falconry'. بودی is the past form of بودن 'was' and together بودی نیاز (inverted order) yields 'was in need' (نیاز = 'need'). The construction therefore means he was always in need of his hawk/falcon.
segments.s740-g23: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (used here to mean 'someone/a certain person'). دفتری is 'a clerk/office attendant' (the agent). دید is past 'saw'. پیش اندرش is a locative phrase: پیش = 'in front / before / in the presence of' and اندر + ش = 'in/within + his' (اندرش = 'before him' / 'in his presence'). The whole phrase says 'a certain clerk saw (it) before him.'
segments.s740-g24: نوشته is the noun 'writing/text' (or 'that which is written'). کلیله is the title/name 'Kalila' (the text's title). بران is a poetic prepositional/adverbial 'upon that / thereon'. دفترش = دفتر + -ش 'his book'. Together: 'a writing (titled) Kalila upon that book of his.'

sentences.s740-s33: He would send those same men, and the attendants filled the whole palace with wealth of boundless dinars.
sentences.s740-s34: At their time there was peace and food; their guards were forty men.
sentences.s740-s29: They kept taking a count of them, for the number of prowlers exceeded three thousand.
sentences.s740-s30: All the palaces, each with its own counsel, took a different course — and what was harmful to the king proved effective.
sentences.s740-s31: From garments and from food, and from gifts as well as from things to be spread out.
sentences.s740-s32: They adorned their porticoes and sought attendants and servants.
sentences.s740-s25: The great king did not harm him, for he was a young child and had become (something) noteworthy.
sentences.s740-s26: His laughing soul was filled with pain, and he turned his hall into a prison.
sentences.s740-s27: Also those who had continuously been with him at times sought counsel from him.
sentences.s740-s28: Many others — both chiefs and juniors — who were with him as wardens/attendants.
sentences.s740-s13: In the left hand of that young man was a severed, dry wolf’s claw.
sentences.s740-s14: A cypress, its head like a buffalo’s, stood upright; then he struck this upon that as he wished.
sentences.s740-s15: The mobed’s heart became sorrowful over the affair, because of his play and vain conduct.
sentences.s740-s16: That wolf’s claw too came as an ill omen — (for) the shakh of the ox and the counsel of the brave youth.
sentences.s740-s5: No one was aware of that scheme, for he alone possessed that secret and that was enough.
sentences.s740-s6: When a year came in which Mary died, he handed the golden bedchamber over to Shirin.
sentences.s740-s7: When Shiruyeh reached the age of two‑eight (i.e. twenty‑eight) and above, the years passed by.
sentences.s740-s8: His father brought (or provided) learned men to him so that he might become renowned and full of skill.
sentences.s740-s1: After that the king's greatness increased so much that the sun took the place where the moon had been.
sentences.s740-s2: He spent all day with the Caesar's daughter, and by night he set her as the chief of his bedchamber.
sentences.s740-s3: Because of Maryam, Shirin would be in pain; always, from his jealousy both her cheeks (faces) turned pale.
sentences.s740-s4: At last he gave Shirin poison; that renowned daughter of Caesar's line perished.
sentences.s740-s21: Because of his child his visage grew pale; because of the ways of the world he became filled with sorrow.
sentences.s740-s22: From the words of the man (called) Setāra-Shamr his heart was full of pain and his liver writhed.
sentences.s740-s23: He kept saying, 'How can the Maker of the heavens have fashioned a face like this?'
sentences.s740-s24: When twenty‑three years of his reign had passed, Shiruyeh flared up his mane.
sentences.s740-s17: From the doings of the age a grievous sorrow became severe; from that the noble‑minded child's fate was thrown into turmoil.
sentences.s740-s18: Where had one seen the natal fortune at his birth? Had one heard it in the Dastur and the Ganjur?
sentences.s740-s19: A mobed came up to the mobed and said: 'That is a marvel with that precious pair.'
sentences.s740-s20: It happened quickly: the mobed told that to the king; Khosrow was keeping his gaze on him for me.
sentences.s740-s9: The mobad kept his gaze on him, day and night, joyful at the king's command.
sentences.s740-s10: So it happened that one day the mobad came down from the throne and approached that fortunate man.
sentences.s740-s11: When he again came near Shiruyeh, he was always in need of his hawk.
sentences.s740-s12: A certain clerk saw, before him, a book on which 'Kalila' was written.
